_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Marshall County, AL. Record survival and courthouse history. Marshall County was formed in 1836 from Cherokee cession lands — relatively late by Tennessee Valley standards. Records are largely complete from formation.

previous_courthouses.fate: Warrenton was the original county seat, located in the center of the county on Sand Mountain. Records transferred when the county seat moved to Guntersville in 1868.
Brick courthouse at Guntersville. Replaced by the current 1957 building.

record_losses.cause: County seat move from Warrenton to Guntersville — minor losses during transfer
records_destroyed: Some loose papers may have been lost during the county seat move
record_losses.description: The county seat moved from Warrenton (on Sand Mountain) to Guntersville (on the Tennessee River) in 1868. Bound record books were transferred intact. Warrenton subsequently declined and is now a small unincorporated community. The Civil War disrupted county operations — Marshall County saw guerrilla activity and both Union and Confederate forces moved through the area — but the courthouse was not deliberately destroyed.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Marshall County's records exist from 1836, but the county was formed late by regional standards — Cherokee removal had only occurred two years before. If {ancestor_name} was in this area before 1836, records would be in the pre-formation Cherokee Nation or under adjacent county jurisdictions.
record_losses.source: Alabama Department of Archives and History. Guide to County Records.

deeds.repository: Marshall County Probate Office, Guntersville; ADAH microfilm

wills_estates.gaps: Minor gaps in earliest loose papers

marriages.repository: Marshall County Probate Office; ADAH microfilm

court_minutes.gaps: Some disruption during Civil War guerrilla period

vital_records.completeness: Spotty until approximately 1925. Sand Mountain communities had lower registration rates.
vital_records.repository: Alabama Department of Public Health; ADAH

substitute_sources.source_type: TVA land acquisition and resettlement records
substitute_sources.description: Guntersville Dam (1939) created the largest TVA lake in Alabama and displaced families along the Tennessee River in Marshall County. TVA resettlement records document the displacement.
substitute_sources.repository: TVA Archives, Knoxville, TN; National Archives (RG 142); ADAH
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Documents families displaced by Guntersville Lake — names, property, compensation, and resettlement locations.
substitute_sources.source: Tennessee Valley Authority. Land Acquisition Records, Guntersville Dam Project.

substitute_sources.description: Albertville, on Sand Mountain, developed as the commercial center for the plateau. Market records, business directories, and newspaper records document the truck farming economy.
substitute_sources.repository: Albertville Public Library; Marshall County Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: For Sand Mountain families who appeared infrequently in courthouse records, Albertville market and business records may provide documentation.

narrative_hooks: Marshall County was not formed until 1836 — two years after Cherokee removal — so if {ancestor_name} was in this area before then, the records are in Cherokee Nation archives or adjacent Alabama county files.
The county seat moved from Warrenton on Sand Mountain to Guntersville on the river in 1868 — the move symbolized the county's shift from mountain to valley orientation.
If {ancestor_name}'s farm was on the Tennessee River flats near Guntersville, check TVA displacement records — Guntersville Dam drowned valley farmland in 1939.
Sand Mountain families left fewer courthouse records because they owned less property and did less legal business — the absence of records is the record of their economic life.
source_references: Alabama Department of Archives and History. Guide to County Records in the Alabama State Archives. Montgomery: ADAH, 2005.
Tennessee Valley Authority. Land Acquisition Records, Guntersville Dam Project.
Marshall County Historical Society. Collections and Finding Aids.
